_meta.notes: Chapter 0: Introduction / Abakada. Teach-only orientation session (no quizzes). Content to be filled in by Ean.

cards.body: Tagalog uses the Latin alphabet. No new script to learn!
The traditional Filipino alphabet has 20 letters: A Ba Ka Da E Ga Ha I La Ma Na Ng O Pa Ra Sa Ta U Wa Ya.

cards.highlight: What you see is what you say
cards.body: Tagalog is mostly phonetic. Unlike English where 'tough', 'through', and 'though' all sound different, Tagalog letters have consistent sounds.
Three things to know: the rolled R, 'ng' is its own letter (like in 'singing'), and the glottal stop (a quick pause, like 'uh-oh').
Karaoke, boondocks (from bundok - mountain), and sari-sari (variety store) are all Tagalog words that made it into English.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: American soldiers in the Philippines borrowed 'bundok' (mountain) - it became 'boondocks' meaning a remote area.

cards.body: Tagalog has no he/she distinction. 'Siya' means he, she, or they. One word for everyone.
You're about to learn your first Tagalog. Let's go!

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: No quizzes in this lesson. Teach-only orientation.

alam_mo_ba: The Philippines has over 180 languages, but Tagalog (Filipino) is the national lingua franca.
The word 'karaoke' comes from Japanese, but the karaoke machine was invented by a Filipino named Roberto del Rosario.
